topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox, a stark directive from management: I was to onboard Sarah. My stomach tightened instantly. Sarah, with her boundless enthusiasm and frankly, her lack of experience, was going to be a nightmare. I stared at the document, tracing the lines with a finger, a slow, deliberate movement. When the meeting arrived, I offered a curt nod. "I can do it," I managed, the words feeling heavy and slightly forced. Throughout the training sessions, I found myself pausing mid-sentence, re-reading the manual three times, my jaw tight. When Sarah asked a question I’d already answered in excruciating detail, a flicker of impatience crossed my eyes. "That's covered in Section B, Sarah. Please refer back." I felt a distinct clenching in my chest, a refusal to let go of the control I felt over the process. By the end of the week, I felt drained, yet strangely satisfied that I had managed to shepherd her through the necessary steps, even if it had cost me a significant amount of my own energy.

[story 2]

"You'll be mentoring Alex starting next Monday," my supervisor announced, clapping my shoulder with an overly bright smile. I managed a small, tight smile in return, my gaze fixed just over their shoulder. Mentoring. The word sounded like a heavy anchor dropped into my already crowded schedule. I spent the weekend meticulously reviewing every procedural document, cross-referencing every potential pitfall. Monday arrived, and Alex, bless her eager face, was already asking questions. I answered them, yes, but each response felt like pushing against a solid wall. There were moments, especially when Alex proposed an alternative workflow, where I would just hold my breath, waiting for the right moment to pivot back to the established routine. My shoulders tensed visibly under my shirt. I wanted to just delegate, to hand the reins over, but some deep-seated need to ensure everything was executed *my* way kept me rooted to the spot, watching her attempt to navigate the terrain I had charted so carefully.

[story 3]

I sat in the conference room, the presentation slides spread before me, feeling a strange, almost prickly sensation prickling the back of my neck. The task was simple: train the new hire. I took a deep breath, trying to smooth out the uneven rhythm of my breathing. When the trainee started asking basic inquiries, I responded with precision, every answer calibrated and perfectly worded. But beneath the surface of my calm demeanor, a low, simmering tension was building. Every time the trainee deviated from the script, I felt an internal resistance flare up—a sharp, almost physical tightening in my throat. "That approach won't work here," I stated firmly, my voice level but carrying an undeniable finality. I kept my hands deliberately still on the podium, refusing to gesture or soften my tone. I was determined to demonstrate the correct path, no matter how much it chafed against the idea of letting someone else take the lead. It was exhausting maintaining that rigid posture of absolute certainty.
